text: I just found out that Zoom, the tool we’ve used for a few virtual meetups so far, leaves your webcam remotely accessible if you use macOS, even if you “uninstall” Zoom afterward: <https://medium.com/@jonathan.leitschuh/zoom-zero-day-4-million-webcams-maybe-an-rce-just-get-them-to-visit-your-website-ac75c83f4ef5>

root.text: This might be a little off-topic. What do you all use for personal-knowledge-management, note-taking, etc? I'm not happy with my lazy Evernote setup. Just searching around, people mention: Notion, Dynalist, Spacemacs + Org-mode, Vimwiki, Zimwiki, TiddlyWiki, Standard Notes, etc, etc. Anyone wanna share their setup for these or others?

<@UC6997THT> The point of that example was that you’re required to handle the case where the numbers aren’t integers, while in python (sub in your favorite exception-based lang) you’d have exceptions here. You can guard exceptions, but the compiler won’t tell you to.

<@UC6997THT> The point of that example was that you’re required to handle the case where the numbers aren’t integers, while in python (sub in your favorite exception-based lang) you’d have exceptions here. You can guard exceptions, but the compiler won’t tell you to.

Yes but it doesn't actually help anything in the example. If some other code calls it with `10, "5"` that code will just return 0 and now the error is hidden. The example is not a good example of how a type system solve issues. Instead it ends up being an example of how to make the code **silently** fail

<@U8A5MS6R1> Thanks – the ink and switch folks are doing some really cool stuff, and I like their manifesto position very much. The focus on local whenever possible is surely the right technical, privacy, and … well, economic (?) approach. On “economic”, local data and user ownership seems much more likely to reduce data siloing, which is a natural barrier to entry for any kind of centralised system to exploit.

<@UJ6LDMMN0> I also saw the causal tree page too – thank you! Something I’ve not seen with causal trees is how to create stable revision semantics. It’s wonderful for eventual consistency, but I don’t see how to create types in it where I can identify a specific system state and pass that about being sure that everyone will be able to resolve exactly the same configuration that I have identified. I could also hash what I see, but then the state might have changed since the hash was created and surely there needs to be a way to avoid this.

<@UJ6LDMMN0> I also saw the causal tree page too – thank you! Something I’ve not seen with causal trees is how to create stable revision semantics. It’s wonderful for eventual consistency, but I don’t see how to create types in it where I can identify a specific system state and pass that about being sure that everyone will be able to resolve exactly the same configuration that I have identified. I could also hash what I see, but then the state might have changed since the hash was created and surely there needs to be a way to avoid this.

I think it would probably be necessary to build other structures on top of it that could guarantee this, which is probably fine. Perhaps it’s a shame it doesn’t have it out of the box, but then, maybe fully versioned history isn’t quite as important as I imagine.

<@UEQ6M68H0> Do Haskell’s `Either`, or Swifts `Result` capture this? Both let you define total functions where outputs are either a result or an error. Function can take these as arguments too, so it seems like they give a closed arithmetic?

I think this idea of knowledge representation gets generalized with multi agents: <http://www.illc.uva.nl/Research/Publications/Reports/PP-2004-20.text.pdf> Also, found out about Lazzarini's network chains a few days ago:

What got me interested in term rewriting is its extreme modularity. Lots of small definitions that can be written in (almost) arbitrary order makes it suitable for embedding into prose, much like maths notation (see <http://khinsen.net/leibniz-examples/examples/leibniz-by-example.html>).

Just booked my flights for Strangeloop! Maybe we create a #strangeloop-2019 channel if there are enough of us. Very honored and excited to be introducing <@U6LBL2CJ1> and <@U6BRN7A0Y> for Dark's launch. Come join if you'll be there -- RSVP here: <https://darklaunch.splashthat.com/>

attachments.title: Dark Launch - Splash
attachments.text: Come join us after spending the day at Strange Loop for a talk from Steve Krouse (Future of Coding) and the unveiling of Dark.  Free food, drinks and chats about removing accidental complexity from backends, the problems with textual coding, coding with live data, eliminating errors in programming, instant deployment and more.Dark is a holistic language, editor, and infrastructure, for building backend web services. We’re excited to show you what we’ve been working on!Doors and bar open at 6pm. At 7pm we’ll have a short talk from Steve Krouse about his upcoming Whole Code Catalog and Paul Biggar will preview Dark for the first time.

Hi all! Longtime follower and recent contributor on the “robust first” computing effort with <@UKLV35EEM>. My background is working in the industry on large software systems. I’m determined to build life-like robustness into a new computing stack. As a concrete analogy: I like to think of how primitive cellular functions build upon each other to achieve higher level goals. This could be similar to how traditional software functions are composed today. The best part is we get to start from scratch. Removing billions of years of cruft :-) we can build crisp and predictable functions with good reuse. That’s part of the fun and challenge of this work, and we’re definitely still at the beginning. Would love to get to know you all and answer questions!

Edit: I hang out in: <#CL0FBFS8H|robust-computation> and <http://gitter.im/t2tile/> is where most of the real work happens.
Hey folks, I released a blog post about how Dark is designed for continuous delivery. Would love all your thoughts! <https://medium.com/darklang/how-dark-deploys-code-in-50ms-771c6dd60671>

Where can I learn about t2 tile? I'm looking for a quick summary or goals

Hello! I stumbled across a mention of this Slack on Twitter and it seemed very aligned to my interests :slightly_smiling_face:

I'm particularly interested in live programming and developer experience, so I write a lot of lisp and I've been slowly teaching myself smalltalk/pharo.

For my day I work on Hack, the programming language that powers Facebook. We spend a lot of time thinking about type systems on big codebases, so sometimes I'm thinking about static types, and other times I think about dynamic types.
Very interesting and promising! Thank you for sharing this!
Very interesting and promising! Thank you for sharing this!

<@U5TCAFTD3> I don’t have a quick answer for you unfortunately. Our home on the web is still Gitter which is more of an long form chat. There’s the [YouTube video series](<https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC1M91QuLZfCzHjBMEKvIc-A>) perhaps one of the early episodes can answer some things. There’s always <@UKLV35EEM> too.

T2 tackles building real hardware (the tile) that can implement some of the ideas and previous theory. (In no particular order):

* resistance to hardware nondetermism and faults, under arbitrary failure modes
* indefinite scalability
* robustness via artificial life principles.

Admittedly I’m not active with the hardware side.

attachments.text: Extraordinary computing demands extraordinary hardware. The T2 Tile Project is building a new best-effort 'unit' of computing called the 'T2 Tile'. Every wee...

text: I have a question. The versionning of types/functions/package/standard lib looks great, but does it scale well with lots of edits/versions? And what is the "size" of the biggest user's code base managed by dark?

I have a question. The versionning of types/functions/package/standard lib looks great, but does it scale well with lots of edits/versions? And what is the "size" of the biggest user's code base managed by dark?

Hi <@U5TCAFTD3>. The goal of the T2 tile project is to demonstrate a strictly indefinitely scalable computer architecture, and use the resulting system to set an initial benchmark record for the 'Average Event Rate' (AER) that such an architecture can achieve.  Perhaps the fastest way into the goals and concepts is the 2016 AAAI paper (5pg Q&A) at <https://www.aaai.org/ocs/index.php/AAAI/AAAI16/paper/download/11987/12203>
Our biggest users aren’t that big (15 tables, 60 routes), so we will definitely be figuring out scaling as we go.
Our biggest users aren’t that big (15 tables, 60 routes), so we will definitely be figuring out scaling as we go.
Thanks! I'll check it out

Mr. Johnson has got the idea. The whole point of a closed arithmetic is to eliminate IF statements. In the old days of computing, every function would have an error return value, and after each function call would be an IF statement to abort the rest of the computation should an error be found. Over time, people got lazy and started to skip the IF statements, and a million bugs were born. fast forward to today, and nobody wants error codes or to write IF statements, so the current idea is to limp along and at least not crash, often foolishly hoping each function never fails.  The fundamental issue is that many things can fail. Erlang had an interesting attitude about what to do when an intermediate function encounters a failure; just crash the thread and start all over. A unique philosophy, borne of the harsh crucible Erlang was formed in, that of a headless box that has to run for years. For graphical interactive programs however, restarting suddenly is not a comfortable interaction style, so the crash + start over idea doesn't play there.  Haskell's Either construct is merely a wrapper around multiple values and is not the same thing as a closed arithmetic. I am not familiar with Swift's result, but again, these language features are typically tacked on at the end, and are not baked into the core arithmetic like they are in Excel and Beads. Closed arithmetic is a property like being a prime number; it is a mathematical property that is either present or not, and if you don't have it you will pay for that omission sooner or later. I find the Java approach of using Try/Catch to be horrendous as it causes spooky action at a distance, something that bothered Einstein and all people who value sturdiness and understandability.
